clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Acute asthma exacerbation is a sudden worsening of airflow obstruction driven by airway inflammation and bronchospasm.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Inflamed hyperreactive airways constrict and trap air, making ventilation harder and increasing work of breathing.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: minutes to hours, sometimes worsening quickly after a trigger or poor control
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe chest tightness, cough, wheeze, and frightening shortness of breath that can escalate rapidly.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to understand why a small reduction in airway diameter sharply worsens airflow.
The story needs a clear bridge from airflow obstruction to distress and hypoxemia risk.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Inflammatory mediators increase bronchial hyperreactivity and airway edema.
Bronchospasm narrows the airway lumen and impedes expiratory airflow.
Air trapping and increased work of breathing create wheeze, dyspnea, and fatigue.

historyClues: recent trigger exposure or poor control
physicalExamClues: reduced air movement can signal severity
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often worsens over hours but may accelerate quickly in severe attacks

labs.name: pulse oximetry
labs.expectedFinding: may remain normal early but can fall with severe disease

imaging.name: targeted chest imaging when needed
imaging.purpose: evaluate alternate diagnoses or complications, not routine confirmation of asthma itself
imaging.expectedFinding: often normal or nonspecific in uncomplicated exacerbation

diagnosticLogic: Severity is judged clinically first, with bedside measures used when feasible and not delaying treatment.
Response to early bronchodilator therapy helps clarify trajectory and escalation needs.

differentials.whyConsidered: can also produce wheeze and respiratory distress
differentials.whyLessLikely: additional allergic or hemodynamic clues change the story logic

differentials.whyConsidered: also causes obstructive symptoms and wheeze
differentials.whyLessLikely: background disease context and chronic pattern differ

escalation: urgent reassessment for poor response or fatigue
emergency-level support when respiratory failure threatens
notes: Treatment intensity should match severity and response rather than waiting for prolonged deterioration.

evidence.claimText: An acute asthma exacerbation reflects worsening airway inflammation and bronchoconstriction that narrow airflow and increase work of breathing.
Common exacerbation symptoms include shortness of breath, cough, wheeze, and chest tightness.
Severe attacks may show accessory muscle use, difficulty speaking, or markedly reduced airflow.
Clinical severity assessment should guide escalation, and peak flow can help when the patient is able to perform it reliably.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Patients able to cooperate with measurement

evidence.claimText: Initial treatment centers on inhaled short-acting bronchodilator therapy, with systemic corticosteroids added for more significant exacerbations.
Hypoxemia, exhaustion, or poor response to early therapy should prompt emergency escalation and close monitoring.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Patients with severe or refractory symptoms

governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for acute asthma symptom severity and urgent treatment framing.
Use for quick-relief medication and steroid escalation context.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Airway Narrowing Drives Distress
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Tie the mystery clues to airway inflammation and bronchospasm rather than vague shortness of breath alone.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Severity Lives in the Exam
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Show how accessory muscle use and difficulty speaking change the urgency of the case.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Rescue Treatment Must Be Timely
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Bronchodilator rescue and steroid escalation should feel like the action sequence of the climax.

visualAnchors.title: Constricting bronchi
visualAnchors.description: Bronchi visibly narrow as the detectives feel the airflow corridor tighten.

visualAnchors.title: Work-of-breathing tableau
visualAnchors.description: Chest effort and accessory muscle recruitment signal worsening severity.

visualAnchors.title: Rescue bronchodilator showdown
visualAnchors.description: Therapy opens the obstructed airway and restores smoother flow.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Visible distress escalates as airflow obstruction worsens.
Severe exam findings determine rescue urgency.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Acute decompensated heart failure is a worsening heart-failure state marked by congestion, poor perfusion, or both.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: The failing heart cannot maintain adequate circulation without rising filling pressures, so fluid backs up into the lungs and systemic tissues.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: hours to days, often triggered by volume accumulation or another precipitating stressor
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often notice breathlessness, swelling, fatigue, and rapid loss of their normal exertional reserve.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The mystery depends on understanding why congestion can worsen even before the heart fully fails as a pump.
Readers need a visual explanation for pulmonary edema, edema, and organ congestion.

pathophysiology.event: cardiac reserve fails under rising demand or worsening dysfunction
pathophysiology.mechanism: Forward flow drops or filling pressures rise beyond compensation.

pathophysiology.event: fluid accumulates in pulmonary and systemic circuits
pathophysiology.mechanism: Elevated venous and capillary pressures push fluid into tissues.

pathophysiology.event: congestion and poor perfusion complicate treatment decisions
pathophysiology.mechanism: Kidney function, electrolytes, and precipitating causes shape safe therapy.

historyClues: fluid accumulation or precipitating illness may precede presentation
physicalExamClues: orthopnea and neck-vein distention point toward congestion
abdominal fullness may reflect right-sided congestion
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often worsens over days, but severe cases can become urgent quickly

labs.name: renal function and electrolytes
labs.purpose: guide diuretic safety and monitor response
labs.expectedFinding: may worsen as congestion and treatment interact

labs.name: natriuretic peptide testing
labs.purpose: support heart-failure probability in the right clinical context
labs.expectedFinding: often elevated but interpreted with the whole picture

imaging.name: chest imaging
imaging.purpose: assess pulmonary congestion and alternate processes
imaging.expectedFinding: may show pulmonary edema or effusions in decompensation

diagnosticLogic: The team should identify whether congestion, poor perfusion, or both are driving the bedside picture.
Monitoring must follow treatment response, kidney function, and likely precipitating causes.

differentials.whyLessLikely: volume-overload clues and edema shift the logic toward heart failure

differentials.whyConsidered: also causes shortness of breath and chest imaging changes
differentials.whyLessLikely: systemic congestion and orthopnea point elsewhere

firstLine: oxygen and supportive care when needed
escalation: close monitoring for worsening perfusion or shock
adjust therapy to symptoms, kidney function, and hemodynamics
notes: Acute treatment should connect symptom relief to the underlying congestion problem rather than feeling like a generic supportive montage.

evidence.claimText: Acute decompensated heart failure reflects worsening cardiac pump dysfunction or filling pressure that causes congestion and poor forward flow.
Common decompensation symptoms include dyspnea, orthopnea, fatigue, edema, and weight gain from fluid buildup.
Physical findings can include pulmonary congestion, elevated neck veins, peripheral edema, and abdominal swelling when right-sided congestion is prominent.
Evaluation and treatment should follow congestion, renal function, electrolytes, and likely precipitating causes rather than relying on one isolated sign.
Patients admitted with fluid overload are commonly treated with diuretic therapy while the team reassesses symptoms, kidney function, and response.
Treatment also requires identifying and addressing precipitating problems and maintaining longer-term heart-failure therapy when clinically appropriate.

governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for congestion patterns and symptom framing.
Use for fluid-overload treatment and monitoring logic.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Congestion Is the Visible Villain
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Show how rising filling pressure creates the external clues of edema and breathlessness.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: The Exam Reads the Hemodynamics
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Neck veins, crackles, and edema should feel like bedside detective clues.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Diuresis Needs Monitoring
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Treatment should look targeted and monitored, not like fluid magically vanishes without consequence.

visualAnchors.title: Pulmonary congestion field
visualAnchors.description: Fluid creeps into the lung spaces while the detectives track the backed-up route.

visualAnchors.title: Systemic edema signal
visualAnchors.description: Peripheral swelling becomes a visible consequence of venous congestion.

visualAnchors.title: Diuretic pressure release
visualAnchors.description: Treatment relieves congestion while the team watches for kidney and electrolyte fallout.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Hemodynamic failure produces the congestion pattern.
Symptoms and physical findings reinforce a congestion-centered explanation.
Monitoring logic shapes safe diuretic treatment.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Ischemic stroke is a sudden neurologic injury caused by interrupted blood flow to brain tissue.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: A blocked cerebral artery deprives brain tissue of oxygen and glucose, so rapid reperfusion determines how much tissue survives.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: minutes to hours, with outcome tightly linked to time and tissue salvage
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Symptoms strike abruptly, often turning an ordinary moment into a neurologic emergency.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story needs a clear picture of how one blocked route can create focal deficits.
Readers should feel why minutes matter before reperfusion options shrink.

pathophysiology.event: a clot lodges in a cerebral artery
pathophysiology.mechanism: Blood flow to downstream tissue falls sharply.
Neural networks fail in the vascular territory affected by ischemia.
Imaging and treatment eligibility decide whether threatened tissue can be saved.

historyClues: symptoms are abrupt rather than slowly progressive
physicalExamClues: neurologic deficit pattern localizes the affected territory
presentation.typicalTimecourse: neurologic deficits begin suddenly and evolve over minutes to hours

labs.name: point-of-care glucose and baseline labs
labs.purpose: exclude mimics and prepare treatment safely
labs.expectedFinding: supportive rather than diagnostic by themselves

imaging.name: urgent brain imaging
imaging.purpose: distinguish ischemia from hemorrhage and guide treatment
imaging.expectedFinding: must rule out bleeding before thrombolysis

imaging.name: vascular imaging when indicated
imaging.purpose: identify large-vessel occlusion and thrombectomy candidates
imaging.expectedFinding: selected patients show treatable occlusion patterns

diagnosticLogic: Stroke care starts with time, symptoms, and imaging rather than waiting for a long differential workup.
Eligibility for thrombolysis or thrombectomy depends on onset window, imaging, and contraindications.

differentials.whyLessLikely: rapid bedside testing helps separate it
timing and imaging push the diagnosis elsewhere

firstLine: consider reperfusion therapy for eligible patients
escalation: endovascular thrombectomy for selected large-vessel occlusion
intensive monitoring for neurologic decline and complications
notes: The diagnosis and treatment should feel time-critical without overpromising that every patient qualifies for every therapy.

evidence.claimText: Ischemic stroke occurs when a clot blocks blood flow in a brain artery and rapidly threatens brain tissue.
evidence.sourceLabel: NINDS approved therapies for ischemic stroke

evidence.claimText: Typical symptoms appear suddenly and can include unilateral weakness or numbness, speech difficulty, or visual disturbance.
Immediate emergency evaluation is critical because reperfusion therapies depend on time from symptom onset and imaging findings.
Brain imaging is required to determine whether bleeding is present before thrombolytic therapy is given.
Eligible patients may receive intravenous thrombolytic therapy within the treatment window after symptom onset.
evidence.sourceLabel: NINDS approved therapies for ischemic stroke
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Eligible adults with acute ischemic stroke

evidence.claimText: Selected patients with large-vessel occlusion may benefit from endovascular thrombectomy beyond the earliest thrombolysis window when imaging supports salvageable tissue.
evidence.sourceLabel: NINDS approved therapies for ischemic stroke

governanceNotes: Preferred source for symptom onset and emergency-response framing.
Use for imaging-first assessment and reperfusion timing logic.

sourceCatalog.sourceLabel: NINDS approved therapies for ischemic stroke
governanceNotes: Use for thrombolysis and thrombectomy timing context.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: The Clock Starts Immediately
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The detectives should treat time of onset as a major clue, not background paperwork.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Imaging Decides the Safe Path
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The story must show why imaging comes before thrombolytic decisions.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Reperfusion Is Earned and Selective
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Use thrombolysis and thrombectomy as distinct but related action beats.

visualAnchors.title: Blocked cerebral route
visualAnchors.description: A clot halts traffic through a critical brain vessel.

visualAnchors.title: Sudden deficit split-screen
visualAnchors.description: A once-normal action fractures into focal neurologic deficit.

visualAnchors.title: Imaging-before-treatment checkpoint
visualAnchors.description: The detectives must confirm the route is blocked, not bleeding, before the rescue move.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Vascular occlusion creates the focal deficit pattern.
Urgency still requires imaging before certain therapies.
Thrombectomy expands treatment for selected large-vessel occlusions.

diseaseCategory: central nervous system infection / medical emergency

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Bacterial meningitis is a rapidly progressive central nervous system infection that inflames the meninges and can become life-threatening quickly.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Bacteria trigger intense meningeal inflammation, raising intracranial and systemic risk while neurological function deteriorates.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: hours to days, with the most severe cases worsening rapidly
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may move from fever and headache to confusion, neck stiffness, and systemic collapse fast enough that delay changes the outcome.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The detectives need to understand the compartment where inflammation unfolds.

physiologyPrerequisites.topic: brain protection and systemic infection response
physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story connects neurologic findings with the danger of sepsis and swelling.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Infection seeds the protective coverings of the brain and spinal cord.
Inflammatory injury produces headache, stiffness, confusion, and systemic illness.

pathophysiology.event: diagnosis and treatment race against deterioration
pathophysiology.mechanism: Testing helps identify the pathogen, but treatment timing remains urgent.

historyClues: age and organism context can change details
physicalExamClues: neck stiffness and mental-status change shift the mystery toward CNS infection
presentation.typicalTimecourse: can progress quickly enough that delay becomes part of the danger

imaging.name: targeted neuroimaging when indicated
imaging.purpose: evaluate safety concerns before lumbar puncture in selected patients
imaging.expectedFinding: used selectively rather than as a universal first move

pathology.name: cerebrospinal fluid analysis
pathology.purpose: characterize meningitis and support organism identification
pathology.expectedFinding: supports diagnosis when feasible to obtain safely

diagnosticLogic: The team should gather urgent diagnostic data without turning the workup into an excuse for dangerous delay.
Testing supports precision, but the emergency is clinical before it becomes beautifully labeled.

differentials.whyLessLikely: bacterial illness often looks more toxic and urgent

differentials.whyConsidered: can also cause altered mental status and fever
differentials.whyLessLikely: the story logic shifts once meningeal signs and CSF patterns dominate

firstLine: prompt antibiotics when bacterial meningitis is suspected
notes: The story should show the tension between diagnostic precision and not delaying life-saving therapy.

evidence.claimText: Bacterial meningitis is an infection-driven inflammation of the meninges around the brain and spinal cord.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults and children with suspected meningitis

evidence.claimText: Typical symptoms include fever, headache, stiff neck, and altered mental status, though presentation can vary by age and organism.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults and children with suspected meningitis

evidence.claimText: Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency because it can progress quickly to seizures, coma, death, or sepsis-related complications.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults and children with suspected meningitis

evidence.claimText: Diagnostic workup uses laboratory testing for meningitis, including blood cultures and cerebrospinal fluid evaluation when feasible.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults and children with suspected meningitis

evidence.claimText: Treatment uses antibiotics and should begin as soon as possible when bacterial meningitis is suspected.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults and children with suspected meningitis

evidence.claimText: If lumbar puncture must be delayed, treatment should still proceed promptly after appropriate early evaluation rather than waiting passively for later confirmation.
evidence.sourceLabel: CDC meningococcal disease emergency treatment guidance
evidence.sourceLocator: treatment after cultures if LP delayed
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults with suspected acute bacterial meningitis

governanceNotes: Preferred source for emergency presentation, testing, and antibiotic timing.

sourceCatalog.sourceLabel: CDC meningococcal disease emergency treatment guidance
governanceNotes: Use for the medical-emergency framing around meningitis and sepsis.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Emergency Recognition Matters
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The clues should make meningitis feel dangerous before the final label appears.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Testing and Treatment Pull Against Delay
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Show that workup supports the truth but does not justify waiting through a worsening emergency.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Sepsis and CNS Danger Travel Together
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The climax should connect neurologic decline with systemic risk.

visualAnchors.title: Inflamed meningeal boundary
visualAnchors.description: The protective layers around the brain glow with danger as inflammation spreads.

visualAnchors.title: Toxic bedside presentation
visualAnchors.description: Fever, pain, and confusion make the urgency visible before lab labels arrive.

visualAnchors.title: Antibiotic race sequence
visualAnchors.description: The detectives must treat while still piecing together the responsible organism.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Meningeal infection produces the symptom cluster.
Diagnostic workup supports treatment without replacing urgent therapy.
When lumbar puncture is delayed, teams must reconcile diagnostic thoroughness with not delaying antibiotics.

diseaseCategory: inflammatory bowel disease / colonic mucosal inflammation

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Ulcerative colitis flare is a recurrence or worsening of inflammatory colonic disease with bleeding, pain, and urgent bowel symptoms.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Diffuse mucosal inflammation in the colon drives bleeding, diarrhea, pain, and escalating severity in active disease.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: days to weeks, with severe flares sometimes escalating fast enough to require hospitalization
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often experience exhausting urgency, abdominal pain, and bleeding that make the disease feel both visible and relentless.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The mystery depends on seeing why inflamed lining creates bleeding and diarrhea.
Readers need a mechanism for why the colon becomes persistently inflamed.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Immune-driven injury disrupts the lining of the large intestine.

pathophysiology.event: mucosal injury produces bleeding and urgent diarrhea
pathophysiology.mechanism: Inflamed tissue loses normal barrier and absorptive function.

pathophysiology.event: severity drives treatment intensity and hospital need
pathophysiology.mechanism: Complications, dehydration, and heavy inflammation raise urgency.

historyClues: known inflammatory bowel disease history helps frame the case
infection still has to be considered and excluded
physicalExamClues: ongoing bleeding and dehydration clues raise concern for severity
complications: toxic megacolon or perforation in severe disease
presentation.typicalTimecourse: flares often build over days but severe disease can become a hospital problem quickly

labs.name: CBC and inflammatory markers
labs.purpose: assess bleeding burden and systemic inflammation
labs.expectedFinding: can support severity assessment but not define the disease alone

imaging.name: targeted abdominal imaging in severe disease
imaging.purpose: evaluate complications rather than confirm routine flare alone
imaging.expectedFinding: used when severe complication is a concern

pathology.name: endoscopy with biopsy
pathology.purpose: confirm inflammatory colitis pattern and support diagnosis
pathology.expectedFinding: mucosal inflammation and biopsy findings support ulcerative colitis

diagnosticLogic: Known ulcerative colitis does not remove the need to assess severity and rule out mimics or infection when appropriate.
Endoscopy and biopsy anchor the diagnosis, but bedside severity still determines urgency.

differentials.whyConsidered: can also cause diarrhea, pain, and bleeding
differentials.whyLessLikely: testing and background history help sort the pattern

firstLine: hospital care for severe or fulminant disease
notes: Do not flatten treatment into one universal medication; the story should respect severity-based escalation.

evidence.claimText: Ulcerative colitis is an inflammatory disease of the colon that causes mucosal injury and recurrent flares.
evidence.sourceLabel: NIDDK ulcerative colitis symptoms and causes
evidence.applicability: General inflammatory bowel disease teaching baseline

evidence.claimText: Common flare symptoms include diarrhea, rectal bleeding, abdominal pain, and urgency.
evidence.sourceLabel: NIDDK ulcerative colitis symptoms and causes
evidence.applicability: General inflammatory bowel disease teaching baseline

evidence.claimText: Doctors diagnose ulcerative colitis using history, testing, and endoscopy with biopsy while ruling out other causes such as infection.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults with suspected or known ulcerative colitis flare

evidence.claimText: Treatment intensity depends on flare severity, and severe or fulminant disease is often treated in the hospital.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: Adults with moderate to severe flare

evidence.claimText: Aminosalicylates, corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, and biologics are used according to severity and response.
People with ulcerative colitis are generally advised to avoid NSAIDs for pain because these medicines may worsen symptoms.
evidence.sourceLocator: other treatments and medicines to avoid

sourceCatalog.sourceLabel: NIDDK ulcerative colitis symptoms and causes
governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for flare symptoms and disease distribution.
Use for medication tiers and hospitalized severe-flare treatment framing.
Use for endoscopy, biopsy, and infection-exclusion logic.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Mucosal Injury Explains the Symptoms
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Bloody diarrhea should feel like a clue that the colon lining itself is under attack.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Severity Changes the Setting
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The story should distinguish outpatient flare logic from severe hospitalized flare logic.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Diagnosis Needs Confirmation and Exclusion
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Known disease still needs careful evaluation when the bedside picture changes.

visualAnchors.title: Inflamed colonic lining
visualAnchors.description: The detectives see a reddened, friable lining that bleeds when disturbed.

visualAnchors.title: Urgency and bleeding clue trail
visualAnchors.description: Symptoms dominate the patient experience before the diagnosis is named.

visualAnchors.title: Severity ward checkpoint
visualAnchors.description: Escalation depends on how sick and depleted the patient becomes.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Inflammation produces the flare symptom cluster.
Diagnosis and severity assessment together shape treatment setting.
Symptom management choices should avoid medications that worsen disease activity.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis is a painful ischemic event caused by obstructed microvascular blood flow in sickle cell disease.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Sickled red cells impede circulation, producing tissue ischemia, pain, and risk for organ complications.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often sudden, with pain escalating over hours and complications able to appear during the same episode
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients can move quickly from ordinary function to severe pain that dominates movement, breathing, and attention.

physiologyPrerequisites.topic: red blood cell shape and flow
physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to understand why altered cell shape changes circulation so dramatically.
The story must connect blocked flow to pain and to dangerous downstream complications.

pathophysiology.event: red cells sickle and obstruct microvascular flow
pathophysiology.mechanism: Abnormal cell shape and adhesion slow circulation and block small vessels.
Reduced perfusion produces severe pain and loss of normal function.

pathophysiology.event: the team must search for complications beyond pain
pathophysiology.mechanism: Acute chest syndrome, infection, or anemia can change the entire treatment path.

commonSigns: possible chest symptoms if acute chest syndrome develops
historyClues: prior individual pain plans matter to the response
physicalExamClues: location and severity of pain matter, but chest symptoms or anemia clues change urgency
presentation.typicalTimecourse: pain can intensify over hours, while complications may emerge during the same crisis

labs.name: CBC and reticulocyte count
labs.purpose: evaluate anemia burden and marrow response
labs.expectedFinding: helps frame whether severe anemia or aplastic complications are present

imaging.name: targeted chest imaging when symptoms warrant
imaging.expectedFinding: used when chest pain, fever, or breathing symptoms raise concern

diagnosticLogic: The workup should validate the pain crisis while actively looking for the dangerous things that can hide inside it.
Pain intensity alone is not the only clue; respiratory and anemia findings can redirect the case.

differentials.whyConsidered: can also cause focal severe pain in sickle cell disease
differentials.whyLessLikely: systemic context and evaluation alter the story logic

differentials.whyConsidered: may overlap with a pain crisis
differentials.whyLessLikely: respiratory symptoms and chest findings move it into a more dangerous lane

escalation: evaluate and treat acute chest syndrome or severe anemia urgently
hospital-based care for uncontrolled pain or dangerous complications
notes: The story should respect pain as real and urgent while still teaching complication vigilance.

evidence.claimText: A vaso-occlusive crisis happens when sickled red blood cells obstruct blood flow and trigger tissue ischemia and pain.
evidence.applicability: General sickle cell emergency teaching baseline

evidence.claimText: Pain crises can occur without warning and often cause severe pain that may require clinic or hospital treatment.
evidence.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell symptoms and emergencies
evidence.applicability: General sickle cell emergency teaching baseline

evidence.claimText: Evaluation during severe pain must also look for dangerous complications such as acute chest syndrome, serious anemia, or infection.
evidence.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell symptoms and emergencies
evidence.sourceLocator: emergency symptoms and acute chest syndrome

evidence.claimText: Pain-crisis management should be rapid, structured, and based on an individualized pain plan when available.
evidence.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell pain management fact sheet

evidence.claimText: Hydration and supportive care may help, but treatment must stay focused on pain control and complication-directed care rather than assuming pain is the only issue.
evidence.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell pain management fact sheet
evidence.sourceLocator: acute or severe pain crisis support

evidence.claimText: Acute chest syndrome is a medical emergency that changes the pathway from pain-only management to respiratory and transfusion-oriented escalation.
evidence.patientSubgroupApplicability: People with sickle cell disease and chest symptoms

sourceCatalog.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell symptoms and emergencies
governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for pain-crisis symptoms and emergency escalation clues.
Use for vaso-occlusion and complication framing.

sourceCatalog.sourceLabel: NHLBI sickle cell pain management fact sheet
governanceNotes: Use for acute pain-crisis management framing.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Blocked Flow Creates Real Pain
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Anchor the mystery in microvascular obstruction rather than vague suffering.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Pain Crisis Is Not the Whole Differential
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The detectives should keep scanning for acute chest syndrome, anemia, and infection.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Rapid Analgesia Is Part of Good Care
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Pain management should feel like competent urgent treatment, not an afterthought.

visualAnchors.title: Sickled-cell traffic jam
visualAnchors.description: Misshapen red cells clog a narrow vessel route and halt progress.

visualAnchors.title: Pain crisis surge
visualAnchors.description: The body reacts to ischemic pain with immediate distress and loss of function.

visualAnchors.title: Complication fork in the road
visualAnchors.description: Respiratory clues force the detectives to ask whether the crisis is becoming acute chest syndrome.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Microvascular obstruction drives the pain event.
Complication screening is justified because acute chest syndrome is a medical emergency.
Supportive care complements rather than replaces rapid analgesia and complication-directed care.
